Analog cafes, paper books, and in-person dinner parties became status symbols. The most coveted luxury in 2068 is a weekend with no screens, no implants, and no algorithmic suggestions -- just weather and silence.

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: The 'Humanity Probe' project, proposed by the UN in 2064, became the largest cooperative engineering effort in history: 190 nations contributing components, 2 million engineers from 80 countries. The probe carries a chip with the name of every human alive at the time of launch. Nigerian poet Chimamanda Okafor-Achebe wrote the inscription: 'We were here. We wondered. We reached.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The North Atlantic cod fishery, the Peruvian anchovy fishery, and the Southeast Asian shrimp fishery all collapsed within the same decade. Two billion people who depended on ocean protein have been forced onto lab-grown alternatives. Oceanographer Dr. Isabella Cortez-Ruiz recorded the last wild Atlantic bluefin tuna in 2066; the footage shows a single fish circling in empty water, looking for a school that no longer exists.

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: The Global AI Authority, headquartered in Zurich under Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo of Senegal, must approve every AI system exceeding 10^18 parameters before deployment. The approval process takes 18 months on average; tech companies call it 'the Zurich bottleneck.' But there have been zero AI safety incidents under the regime, and Diallo's stock answer to critics is three words: 'You're welcome. Next?'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Council 2.0, expanded to include China, India, and the African Union, manages shipping lanes carrying 30% of global trade, a network of research stations, and 14 marine protected areas. Commissioner Aleksei Volkov signs 400 permits per day from his office in Murmansk, where roses now bloom in the garden outside -- a fact he finds 'beautiful and obscene in equal measure.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: At 2.6C, life is different but functional. Underground cities in the Gulf states house 20 million; floating neighborhoods in Bangladesh accommodate 8 million; and Canada's population has doubled as climate migrants resettled the warming prairies. Danish architect Sofie Andersen, who designed floating Copenhagen, shrugs: 'Humans adapted to the Ice Age. We'll adapt to this. We just won't enjoy the process.'

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The 'Coalition of the Willing' model has become permanent: 40 to 60 nations form temporary alliances around specific problems -- climate, pandemics, asteroid defense -- then dissolve and reform. It's messy, slow, and somehow works. Brazilian diplomat Dr. Carolina Machado, who has brokered seven such coalitions, compares it to 'jazz: no conductor, no sheet music, but when it works, it's the most beautiful sound in the room.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: Seven billion humans -- a number that would have seemed impossibly large in 1970 and impossibly small in 2050. Every year, roughly 90 million die and 60 million are born; the gap widens each year. Real estate prices in most cities have been falling for a decade. Japanese artist Haruki Yamamoto's installation '30 Million Empty Chairs' -- one for each classroom seat that will never be filled -- spans an entire prefecture.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: The asteroid mining conglomerate Orbital Resources Inc. posted $800 billion in revenue in 2074, making CEO Hiroshi Tanaka-Williams the wealthiest human in history. Lunar tourism serves 50,000 visitors annually at $200,000 per ticket, and the zero-gravity manufacturing platform 'Forge-7' produces semiconductor crystals of a purity impossible on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's a factory floor with a view.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The US share of global GDP fell below 10% in 2073 for the first time since 1870. The Navy can no longer maintain two-ocean patrols; the carrier USS Gerald Ford was decommissioned and sold to India for scrap. A wistful graffiti message appeared on the Lincoln Memorial: 'We were something once.' Park ranger Miguel Santos scrubbed it off by morning, but someone photographed it first.

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: Central Africa, Central Asia, and the Pacific Islands serve as battlegrounds for great powers fighting through autonomous drone swarms and local militias. The 'Invisible Wars' generate no headlines in the capital cities that sponsor them; Congolese journalist Grace Mwamba's documentary 'Someone Else's War' -- showing AI-guided weapons striking a school in Goma -- was viewed 3 billion times and changed nothing.
events.narration: The Last Great War of 2078-2081, as historians would later name it, emerged not from direct confrontation between the major powers but through their competing autonomous military systems deployed across Central Asia and sub-Saharan Africa. Unlike the proxy conflicts of the previous century, human soldiers rarely engaged directly; instead, Chinese, European, and Brazilian AI-guided forces fought through increasingly sophisticated unmanned platforms while their sponsoring nations maintained diplomatic relations. The war's most distinctive feature was its containment within predetermined theaters—a grim testament to the international governance structures that had evolved since 2070, which paradoxically enabled systematic destruction in designated zones while preserving global commerce elsewhere. The conflict would ultimately claim 2.3 million lives, primarily among civilian populations caught between competing algorithmic strategies, before exhaustion of military manufacturing capacity forced a negotiated settlement in early 2081.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: Atmospheric CO2 has fallen from a peak of 480 ppm to 390 and is still dropping. The rewilded Amazon -- 3 million square kilometers of regenerated forest planted by Brazilian-led autonomous systems -- absorbs 4 gigatons annually. Dr. Ndlovu allowed herself an uncharacteristic smile at the podium: 'The planet is healing. Slowly. Grudgingly. But the direction is right.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Global population: 6.8 billion, down from a peak of 9.7 billion in 2064. Every continent is shrinking. Cities are consolidating: Detroit merged with three neighboring towns; Shanghai closed 200 schools in a single year. Economist Dr. Nneka Obi-Fernandez argues this is the most profound challenge humanity has ever faced: 'We built a civilization that requires growth. Now we must learn to shrink gracefully, or not at all.'

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: Universal Basic Income of $2,400/month (in 2085 dollars) is paid to every human on Earth, funded by a 4% global wealth tax and AI productivity dividends. Markets still function, billionaires still exist (though the richest human 'only' controls $400 billion), and the startup culture thrives in Lagos, Bangalore, and Buenos Aires. Swedish economist Dr. Erik Lindgren calls it 'capitalism with a conscience -- 60 years late, but better than never.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The 'Grey Goo Scare' of 2083 -- when a self-replicating nanotechnology experiment consumed 4 square kilometers of Australian outback before being contained -- introduced a risk category that didn't exist a decade earlier. Combined with the emergence of AI systems that communicate in languages humans can't parse and quantum computing capabilities that could break all encryption simultaneously, the threat landscape has shifted in ways the old risk models can't capture.

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: The 'Bio-Sovereign' movement won: 85% of humans are unmodified, and the Universal Declaration of Biological Integrity (2079) enshrines the right to an unaltered genome. Enhanced individuals exist but are socially marginalized, clustered in 'acceleration enclaves' in Singapore, Dubai, and off-world habitats. Baseline human Marta Kowalski of Warsaw takes quiet pride in her unmodified body: 'My great-grandmother survived Auschwitz in this same DNA. I'm not letting anyone edit it.'

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: Dr. Aisha Patel's quantum vacuum energy drive, first tested in 2068, has been scaled to propel a 100-ton vessel at 40% lightspeed. The implications rewrote every assumption about humanity's future: Alpha Centauri in 11 years, TRAPPIST-1 in 95. Patel, now 50 and the most famous scientist alive, is designing the first crewed interstellar vessel from her lab in Leicester. She keeps a poster of the Voyager probe on her wall: 'That was humanity crawling. We're about to walk.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: Dubai's vertical quantum computing district employs 300,000 engineers; Beirut has become the Mediterranean's cultural capital; and the Israeli-Palestinian Federation, improbably, works. GDP per capita across the region exceeds the global average for the first time in history. Saudi novelist Hanan al-Mutairi's 'The Second Nahda' -- comparing the transformation to the 19th-century Arab Renaissance -- won the Nobel Prize in Literature.

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: The 'Concert of Nations' model -- 190 sovereign states cooperating through 40 specialized agencies on climate, AI safety, biodefense, and space -- is messy, redundant, and remarkably effective. French diplomat Dr. Claire Beaumont, who has brokered agreements on three continents, compares it to 'a marriage: nobody is happy, everyone complains, and somehow the dishes get done.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: The 'Separation Doctrine,' written into the constitutions of 140 nations, maintains a hard boundary: AI manages infrastructure, logistics, and data; humans make all decisions involving ethics, art, relationships, and governance. The boundary is enforced by law and reinforced by culture. A child in 2095 knows instinctively what is 'AI work' and what is 'human work' the way a child in 2000 knew the difference between indoor and outdoor voices.

events.title: China 2095
events.description: The entity that calls itself China in 2095 bears no resemblance to the People's Republic. It is governed by an AI-human hybrid council, uses a post-monetary allocation system, and has abolished both private property and state ownership in favor of 'commons stewardship.' Its citizens rate their life satisfaction at 8.4 out of 10. Political scientists from every other system study it obsessively and agree on only one thing: they cannot categorize it.

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: We nearly lost it all in the 2070s -- the war, the pandemic, the climate collapse. Twenty years later, the scars are visible but the patient is walking again. The rebuilt city of Taipei hosted the Global Recovery Summit. New institutions were built from the rubble of the old. The birth rate is rising for the first time in decades, and a Bangladeshi journalist asked Dr. Osei if she was optimistic. She replied: 'Optimism is for people who haven't seen what I've seen. What I have is something harder: evidence that we can break and then get up.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: The 'Copenhagen Standard' -- universal healthcare, AI-assisted education, 24-hour work weeks, and guaranteed creative sabbaticals -- has been adopted by 60 nations. Europeans live an average of 114 years, work less than any society in history, and produce more art per capita than any civilization ever has. The criticism, delivered by American economist Dr. James Chen, is also telling: 'Europe solved every problem except boredom.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: The Climate Maintenance Authority celebrates its 30th anniversary in 2095. Its stratospheric aerosol fleet, orbital mirror array, and ocean alkalinity program hold temperatures at +1.8C -- but the system runs on a $5 trillion annual budget and can never be turned off. Dr. Sow described it as 'the most expensive life-support machine in history, keeping a planet alive that we nearly killed. The bill comes due every year, forever. Our grandchildren will pay it, and their grandchildren after them.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: No world government exists, yet somehow the climate is managed, pandemics are contained, and asteroid defense operates flawlessly -- all through overlapping coalitions that form, act, and dissolve like weather patterns. Brazilian diplomat Dr. Carolina Machado, now 80 and the architect of the system, compares it to 'an orchestra without a conductor that somehow plays in tune. Don't ask me how. I designed it and I still don't fully understand why it works.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Three point eight billion. The century's catastrophes -- the pandemic of 2081, the nuclear exchange of 2078, the climate famines of the 2080s -- each took hundreds of millions. But the deepest cut was the simplest: people stopped having children. Not from despair, but from a quiet, global exhaustion that no policy could reverse. Dr. Eze's final report contained a single graph showing population from 10,000 BCE to 2095 CE. The peak and fall look like a wave breaking. She did not add commentary.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Mars: 12,000 residents across four cities. Moon: 25,000 across six settlements. Orbital habitats: 50,000 across twenty stations. Ceres mining colony: 3,000. Total off-Earth population: 90,000 and the first generation born entirely in space has children of its own. When asked by a journalist if he considered himself human, 14-year-old Mars-born Tomás Vasquez-Liu replied without hesitation: 'Of course. Just not the kind that needs a blue sky.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: The US commands the third-largest economy, the second-largest military, and the first-largest cultural export industry on Earth. It is one great power among four, and the adjustment from 'indispensable nation' to 'important nation' has taken 50 years and still stings. Ambassador David Chen-Washington tells foreign colleagues: 'We used to run the show. Now we're in the band. Turns out we play pretty well when we're not trying to conduct.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: A person from the year 2000, transported to 2099, would understand nothing. Three species of human coexist. Half the economy is managed by an AI that communicates in mathematics. A quarter of the population exists as digital consciousness. Nation-states share the planet with corporate polities and virtual nations. The climate is managed by orbital mirrors. And the most popular New Year's Eve tradition is a moment of silence for the old world -- the one with polar bears, cash registers, and a single internet -- that exists now only in the memories of uploaded minds who chose to remember it.
